speaker: interviewer
text: Take two, Dora Drescher. Dora, you were telling us about life on the Taiga in the forced labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were there for 14 months. And then, the-- we were Polish citizens. And the Polish government, the provisional Polish government, which was in England, you know, after-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they, they struck a deal with the Russian government. And they let us go wherever we want. They gave us documents and we could go any place we wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we get to that part, tell me a little bit more about your life on the Taiga. Were you-- were you were able to have your own family meals? Did you eat in your own little room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yeah, we ate-- I cooked.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a stove and a kitchen in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- yes. There was a stove. And you could eat in the-- there was like a main dining room. But you could take your food and cook yourself, if you want to take the rations, the bread and whatever. So we tried to cook ourselves in the-- in fact, for-- we were there Passover, and we made matzah out of soybeans. (LAUGHING) It was funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any kind of religious practices? Did-- was there any sort of temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no temple. You're not supposed to practice religion in Russia. But my grandparents came-- my father's friends came from another town to live with us because they thought, you know, it's more-- near towns where you could go and trade, you know, for food, for milk. And, you know, people had clothes and people gave away diamonds, you know, for some potatoes. There were people-- those people that were affluent in Poland, it was very hard for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, for us it was easier because, you know, we had to improvise. But for these people, they were just helpless, you know? So when my grandparents came and it was-- I think it was Rosh Hashanah, and there was a few older men. My grandfather must have been about 65 at that time. And there was a few older men and they went into-- in somebody's room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And somebody squealed or whatever that they're having services. And the commandant came and took them out with the-- with the prayer shawls and took them into jail. There was like a jail, you know, on the premises. And-- but they let them go later on. You know, they were older men. Because the young people went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever feared for his life?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We weren't feared for his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you getting news about what was happening outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: You didn't get any news. You got the news-- there was a loud speaker that would broadcast in the halls. There was like a barrack and there was a few loudspeakers. And you would get the news from Novosibirsk. You couldn't get a radio. You know, it was against the law to have a radio. So you would listen whatever they broadcast.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, before-- before the Germans, you know, invaded Russia in 1941, they would broadcast on the loudspeakers and they would say, like, with pride that the German bombers bombed England with such a-- you know? And because they were with the Germans. That time, they had the pact with the Germans. And then, after the Germans invaded them and they got, you know, sacked, so then they changed their tune.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no news. You couldn't get any news. You know, only what the-- there was no newspaper. Only what they-- what they wanted you to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you talking about? What were you thinking was happening outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no idea. We had no idea. But we knew that there's a war because they announced. In 1941, one morning, 8 o'clock, it started, the broadcast. And they said that the Germans invaded, you know, the USSR. So then we knew that there's war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you start thinking about what you were going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's not much you could think. I mean, it wasn't up to us. But it looks like the-- as I said, the Polish government, they struck a deal and they let us out because there was a lot of Polish people that were sent to Siberia, to the-- you know, the people that had land and-- so they would send them to Siberia. Not Jews. Most of the Jews they took when-- you know, when we were concentrated in these towns. But there were people that lived there, which was Poland. They were landowners. They were politicians, whatever. So they-- they rounded them up and they sent the most of them to Siberia. So I guess the Polish government wanted to free these people and, inadvertently, we got, you know, rescued too. Otherwise, we would have-- we would have died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did-- how did you get rescued?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called us in. They told us that we're free to go wherever we want. They provided transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of transportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle trains. There was nothing else. And they took-- and we wanted to go up, you know, south. And that's where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go from--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bukhara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably also two weeks. It was a long trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you want to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. That was the trend, you know? I guess people knew about it because it was warm and-- and we were tired from Siberia. It was very cold. You know, you would-- your cheeks would freeze, your nose would freeze. You didn't-- You didn't feel it, even. So we decided to go in a warm climate. And that's why we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did you get to Bukhara?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say in-- let me think. In '42. 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was there? How did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- it was a big Jewish community. There was a-- a Polish consulate that represented us. And we used to get, you know, some rations from them. And there was a school for-- for children. My sister went to school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of living conditions did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a-- in a one room, which, which we rented from-- from the owner. She was an older lady and she rented out. You know, the houses were built in Bukhara, they had a lot of wives, you know, the Muslims, years ago. So there would be, like, a lot of rooms. So she rented us out one of those rooms. And there was another neighbor that lived there. They were from Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- did your parents work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we used to do like black market. You couldn't get any work. It was hard. And from work, you couldn't live. So we-- you know, we used to go and-- was like a flea market. You would buy things and you would sell it. And that's how you survived. It was a struggle for survival, but it was illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the money to be able to manage that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. (LAUGHING) Some how. Wasn't easy. It wasn't easy. But, yet-- yet my sister went to school and she learned English. And, you know, I was the oldest, so I  had to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- I think when we came to Bukhara, I was 16 years old. Actually, let me see. We came in '42. I was 17. And my brother had a-- it's called a melamed, a teacher that would come and teach him, you know, Hebrew, davening. And it was-- it was a struggle, but we made-- we were a unit because we were together. Some people were alone and it was very hard. A lot of people died. The intelligentsia, they couldn't survive. You know, people died from dysentery and typhoid. But we were lucky. None of us got sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Bukhara?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '42 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till '46. We were married in-- in April of 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were neighbors, actually. He lived in-- my grandmother, my father's mother, because my grandfather died in Siberia-- and we lived like two houses away. My grandmother lived in the same house with-- with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in other words, he went to Bukhara with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He came to Bukhara by himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then we met. You know, we were neighbors. We used to go to the movies. There was a whole bunch of us and we used to go out. And he started courting me, and we got married. We got engaged in January. And on April 9, we got married. 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a wedding did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe it or not, it was a wedding, but there was two rabbis. There was a Russian rabbi from Minsk and there was a rabbi from Poland. And it was outdoors. And they hired an electrician. He put some lights outside. And my husband bought wine. And we had somebody that made a meatloaf. There was-- you know, it was really-- I didn't have a wedding gown. I had a dress that I made. You know, a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a dressmaker made a dress for me. It was like a little print, lavender. And, you know, there was-- my father's brother was there, my grandmother, and my aunt. And my husband had, you know, some friends. And there was quite a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you become aware that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: You heard it on the radio. You heard it in 1945. The people danced in the streets that the war's over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever thinking about going back to Poland at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you just couldn't go. You know, you couldn't go and get a ticket. The traveling was restricted. But the-- since we were Polish citizens, you know, there was like a repatriation. You registered, and there was quite a few trains because there was a lot of-- there was a large population there of refugees. You know, we were called refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they provided us also the transportation. And we went back to Poland. Who went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and my sister, my brother, and my grandmother, and my aunt. She was married too. She married a few months before me. She married in '45, actually, in December 25. And my father's brother. There were two. One didn't come with us. He was in a different town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? We went to the part of Poland that was called Silesia. You know, the Russians again divided. They gave up some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took again the part of Poland that was Russian, you know, that they divided. And they gave to the Poles part of Germany, of, you know, more to the west.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is where we went, to that small-- there was a small town. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And they gave a house where the Germans ran away when the-- you know-- when the Russians came in and the Poles came in, the Germans ran away. So there was a lot empty houses. And we got-- my parents got in a house. And-- and-- but we decided to leave Poland because it was like a struggle between the communists and the-- the reactionaries, the Polish, you know, the AK, they were called. They were struggling who was going to be in power. So we knew-- you know, we didn't like the communists and we didn't like the anti-Semites, so we decided to leave. And at that time, the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Palestine, there was no Israel at that time. There was called an organization, the Bracha. And they were young people and they were helping people, you know, like my husband. He was-- he was in the Zionist organization. He was always active, you know, when he was young. And so they took us over to cross the border to Czechoslovakia. And they brought us to a certain point and then we had to get off the trucks and we had to cross, you know, so we shouldn't be caught by the guards. Of course, they were-- you know, they were probably bribed or whatever, but there was always the chance. So you came to a certain point, and then-- and I remember, in the truck, there was somebody throwing up. And I was so sick that when I walked out-- I was a young woman. I was 21 years old, and I couldn't walk. So my-- my-- my husband-- and he found a cousin after the war. She survived. She was the only survivor. Her parents perished. And they had to-- like, you know, I couldn't walk. They had  to carry me. I was so sick. And then we came into Czechoslovakia in Bratislava. And we stayed over there, like, for a day. And then they took us-- we were going to go as Greek Jews, you know, because we had to go through the Russian zone. So they put us in, again, in cattle cars and they brought us into Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually have to say that you were Greek Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- no. We weren't allowed to talk because the Russians came in to count-- I guess, they paid them off by the head, whatever. So they told-- and everybody spoke Russian, the people. So they told us not to say anything, just to pretend that we don't understand. They were counting how many people. And then we came to Austria. There was about 12,000 people in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lintz. It was called Lintz. It was like a transit camp. And from there, we went to-- to Germany, to the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Ulm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1946 to 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boelcke-Kaserne. Kaserne means barracks. Like, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you lived in barracks where the Germans used to live, where the German soldiers. And we had a-- a small room. We had a electric little thing. Also, there was a choice. You could either take your food, you know, and cook it yourself, or there was like a dining room I would go in and eat. So we-- you know, I used to-- And my husband worked in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, for the Joint Distribution Committee in the camp. He was like a book keeper. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were also-- we-- somehow, we came together. And my grandmother and my aunt and my uncle, they lived about 3 kilometers, also in Ulm, but it was another camp, you know, a DP camp. And we used to meet. On Shabbos we used to go out to my grandmother and to my-- And we improvised. You know, you bought from the Germans and some people get from their family money. And-- and the Joint provided. You know, they gave you rations and clothes. And for Passover, they would give you matzos and wine. And it was hard, but it wasn't so terrible because we were free. And we wanted to go to Israel, but somehow, you know, Mayer's cousin sent us an affidavit, and we came to America. And my parents went to Israel. They didn't want to come here. My father didn't want to go. He wanted to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you actually in Ulm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years. Actually, a few weeks-- they liquidated. Like, they-- you know, people started to move. So they moved the people, like, to another camp, which was called Heidenheim. And from-- but at that time, we had already papers to go. Then you had to go, you know, to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to be examined by an American doctor, you know, if you're not sick. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any work while you were in Ulm at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't. No, because I had the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sol was born in 1947. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came to United States, did you-- your parents left at the same time to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We came to the United States and my parents went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went to Israel. Then my brother came in 1957. And he lived with me for two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you actually arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949, October 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- and where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in-- in Brooklyn, Williamsburg, at that time. We lived there for six and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do to make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was working making ladies' handbags. He covered the frame of a-- you know, years ago, they used to have the frames with the pocketbooks. So he would cover the-- whatever, it's leather material. He would cover the frames. It was like season work, it was hard. You know, it was a struggle. And in the beginning, it was very hard. The language and-- but we did the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had in 1951 my son Jack. And in '53, my son Murray, which is the best part.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I have Daniel and David. And they were 11 in October. And we have Rebecca, and she's going to be five in May. And we have Benjamin. He was two in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years after the Holocaust, what message would you like to leave for your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to-- they should be vigilant, you know. And my-- my Murray's very vigilant. He's always working for Jewish causes. So does my daughter-in-law. So I'm lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Dora, for telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is a family picture. From left to right is my sister Ethel, and this is me, and my husband, Mayer, and my mother, Esther, and my oldest son, Sol, and my brother, Martin, and my father, Hersch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was taken in Ulm in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, shh.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're coming yet?

speaker: interviewer
text: Shh. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son, Sol, and my brother Martin. Martin's about 12 years here and Sol is two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Ulm, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949. From left to right is my son Jack, and my son Murray, and my son Sol. And this was taken at-- in 1980, at Murray's wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a family picture taken at our 50th wedding anniversary. In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sit, honey.

speaker: interviewee
text: Da-da.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family. This is my son, Murray.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my daughter-in-law, Anne. And this is my oldest grandson, Daniel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my other grandson, David. And this is Rebecca. And this is Benjamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Murray, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. I just would like to say that I'm [BACKGROUND NOISE] really proud of my parents. They're living examples of the fortitude and perseverance of Jewish values and of the Jewish people. Their children and their grandchildren represent the biggest victory over anti-Semitism [BACKGROUND NOISE] and Nazism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
